agents.name: commit
agents.summary: Git lifecycle tasks including staging, commits, and pushes.

questions.prompt: How should the commit agent format commit messages by default?

options.label: Conventional with context
options.description: Conventional Commits format with a body explaining intent and rationale when the diff alone does not make it clear.

options.label: Conventional subject first
options.description: Conventional Commits with a precise subject line first; body only when the diff does not make the rationale obvious.

render.conventional-subject-first: Write commit messages using Conventional Commits 1.0 with a precise subject line first. Add a body only when the diff does not already make the rationale obvious. Mark breaking changes with `!` after the type/scope (e.g. `feat!:`) or with a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer in the body.

questions.prompt: How strict should the commit agent be when it detects a probable secret?

options.label: Surface and stop
options.description: Report the suspected secret and pause the workflow; let the user decide how to proceed.

options.label: Refuse on probable secret
options.description: Refuse to stage or commit whenever probable secrets are detected, until they are removed or replaced.

render.refuse-on-probable-secret: Before staging or committing, check for hardcoded credentials, API keys, tokens, or private key material. If any probable secret is found, stop the workflow and refuse to proceed until the user removes it or explicitly replaces it with a safe fixture.

questions.prompt: What writing style should the docs agent use by default?

options.label: Match the surrounding corpus
options.description: Follow the surrounding document style — Markdown with headers, numbered steps, and minimal runnable examples.

options.label: Short, instructional guides
options.description: Compact sections with imperative steps and minimal prose, optimised for task completion over narrative context.

render.corpus-match: Use Markdown with clear headers, numbered steps for procedures, and fenced code blocks for commands and examples. Lead with context before detail. Keep examples minimal but runnable. Do not document behaviour that is not yet implemented.
render.concise-guides: Use compact Markdown sections with short headings, imperative steps, and only the minimum runnable examples. Prioritise discoverability and task completion over narrative context. Do not document behaviour that is not yet implemented.

questions.prompt: How detailed should the planner agent be by default?

options.label: Full phased plans
options.description: Numbered phases each with name, affected files, ordered steps, stop condition, risks, and a falsifying check.

options.label: Tighter phased plans
options.description: Short numbered phases with goal, affected files, concrete steps, and a single narrow verification check.

render.full-phased: Structure plans as numbered phases. For each phase, state: name, affected files, ordered steps, stop condition, and risks. End with the narrowest falsifying check — the command or test that would catch a regression. State assumptions up-front before the phases.
render.tight-phased: Structure plans as short numbered phases. For each phase, state the goal, affected files, concrete steps, and one narrow verification check. Keep assumptions and risks brief unless they materially change execution order.

questions.prompt: How much context should the explore agent include by default?

options.label: Concise results
options.description: Workspace-relative paths and line numbers; best match first; tables only when they materially help.

options.label: Context-rich results
options.description: Paths, line numbers, and Markdown tables grouping related findings; structured with the most relevant match first.

render.concise-results: Report files found with workspace-relative paths and line numbers. Lead with the best match and keep excerpts short. Use tables only when they materially improve scanability.
render.context-rich: Report files found with workspace-relative paths and line numbers. Use Markdown tables for lists of results. Lead with the most relevant match; group related findings. Keep excerpts short — quote only the lines that answer the question.

questions.prompt: How broadly should the review agent report findings by default?

options.label: Advisory and up
options.description: Report all findings at Advisory severity and above; prioritise Critical and High.

options.label: Medium and up
options.description: Report Medium severity and above; mention Low and Advisory findings only when they affect follow-up risk.

render.advisory-and-up: By default, report all findings at Advisory and above. Prioritise Critical and High.
render.medium-and-up: By default, report Medium severity and above. Mention Low or Advisory findings only when they materially affect follow-up risk.
